turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has the opinion of the Court in case 16-1215, Lamar, Archer & Cofrin v. Appling.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Respondent Appling owed petitioner Law Firm Lamar legal fees. To induce Lamar to continue their representation of him, Appling told Lamar he would receive a tax refund large enough to pay his debt to the firm. The tax refund Appling received however was less than what he had represented and he used it to pay other debts. Eventually Lamar sued Appling over the unpaid bills and obtained a judgment against him. After Appling filed for bankruptcy, Lamar sought to avoid the discharge of its debt under 11 U.S.C. Section 523(a)(2)(A), which bars the discharge of any debt from money, property, services or credit to the extent obtained by false pretenses or false representation or actual fraud, other than a statement supporting the debtor's financial condition. If the statement is one respecting the debtor's financial condition, it must be in writing for the associated debt to be nondischargeable. This suit sounds boring, doesn't it? This suit is about what constitutes a statement respecting the debtor's financial condition. Does a statement about a single asset qualify, or must the statement be about the debtor's overall financial status? The answer matters to the parties, because the false statements at issue concerns a single asset, Appling's tax refund and were made orally. So if the single asset statements here qualify as respecting a debtor's financial condition, there is no bar to discharge, because the statements were not made in writing. If however the statements fall into the more general category of false pretenses, false representation or actual fraud, for which there is no writing requirement, then the associated debt will be deemed nondischargeable. As dry as this issue might sound, it generated a split among the Courts of Appeals. We granted certiorari to resolve the conflict as to whether a statement about a single asset can be a statement respecting the debtor's financial condition. Like the Court of Appeals for the Eleventh Circuit below, we hold that it can and affirm. Our decision rests on the plain language of the statute and the ordinary meaning of respecting. The parties agree that financial condition means one's overall financial status. The issue is whether respecting when used in a legal context like this one has a broadening effect, ensuring that the scope of a provision covers not only its subject, but also matters relating to that subject. We conclude that it does. A statement thus is respecting a debtor's financial condition if it has a direct relation to or impact on the debtor's overall financial status. A single asset has a direct relation to and impact on aggregate financial condition. So, a statement about a single asset bears on a debtor's overall financial condition and can help indicate whether a debtor is solvent or insolvent and able to repay a given debt or not. Naturally then a statement about a single asset can be a statement respecting the debtor's financial condition. As we explained in our opinion, if we were to instead accept petitioner's view, which is that a statement respecting the debtor's financial condition means only a statement that captures the debtor's overall financial status, incoherent results would obtain. A highly generalized statement like I am above water would need to be in writing to foreclose discharge, whereas a highly specific statement like, I have $200,000 of equity in my house, enough to pay your bill, would not. Likewise, a misrepresentation about a single asset made in the context of a balance sheet would trigger the writing requirement, but the exact same misrepresentation made on its own or in the context of a list of some but not all of the debtor's assets and liabilities would not. That makes no sense. Finally, the statutory history of the phrase, statement respecting the debtor's financial condition, corroborates our reading of the text. For these and other reasons set forth in our opinion, we affirm the judgment of the Court of Appeals for the Eleventh Circuit. The decision of the Court is unanimous. Justice Thomas, Justice Alito and Justice Gorsuch joined all, but Part 3(b) of the opinion.
